1.synthesis: Paul opens with an unusually full self-description for a letter to a trusted co-worker: he is a servant of God and apostle of Jesus Christ for the sake of the faith of God's elect and their knowledge of the truth that accords with godliness — in hope of eternal life, which God who never lies promised before the ages began and manifested in due time through the preaching entrusted to Paul by the command of God our Savior (vv.1–3). To Titus, his true child in a common faith: grace and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Savior (v.4). The compressed prologue locates the letter's practical instructions within the largest possible theological frame: apostolic authority, electing grace, promised eternal life, and the revealed word.
1.historical_context: Chrysostom commends Titus as an approved companion of Paul: he would not have committed to him the charge of that whole island, nor would he have given him jurisdiction over so many bishops, unless he had been tried and found faithful. Crete was a large Mediterranean island with a reputation for cultural and moral disorder — Paul's later quotation of the Cretan poet Epimenides confirms that the context was genuinely difficult. Gill on eternal life as the purpose of the apostolic ministry: the Gospel ministry was designed to bring God's elect to hope of eternal glory and happiness, out of their natural state where they are without any such hope. The triple description of Paul's apostleship as for the faith of the elect, the knowledge of the truth, and the hope of eternal life maps the scope and purpose of everything Titus is being left to continue.
1.literary_structure: The prologue is architecturally significant: each element grounds the next. The apostleship grounds the authority; the faith and knowledge of the elect ground the purpose; the hope of eternal life grounds the motivation; the pre-temporal promise of God grounds the certainty of that hope; the timely manifestation through preaching grounds the present mission. The greeting to Titus as true child in a common faith places Titus within the same chain of transmission: what Paul received and preached, Titus received and must now guard. The common faith (not merely Paul's faith) is the shared apostolic deposit belonging to all God's elect.
1.theological_application: Gill on God who cannot lie as the guarantor of eternal life: the promise was made before the ages began — before time, in the eternal counsel of the Triune God — and its fulfillment through historical proclamation in due time demonstrates that the entire sweep of redemptive history is the outworking of a pre-temporal design. The knowledge of the truth that accords with godliness is the letter's programmatic statement: true theology produces godly living. This connection between knowledge and godliness governs the practical instructions that follow — sound doctrine about grace produces good works, and doctrine that fails to produce godliness is suspect regardless of its formal correctness.
1.christology: Christ appears both as Jesus Christ the Lord in Paul's commission (v.1) and as God our Savior in the final greeting (v.4) — a title shared with God the Father in v.3 (God our Savior) and explicitly applied to Christ in 2:13 (our great God and Savior Jesus Christ). The letter thus opens by placing Father and Son together as the joint source of grace and peace and the joint ground of the apostolic mission. The eternal life promised before the ages by the God who cannot lie is the same eternal life that Christ Jesus our Savior will secure through his appearing — the beginning and end of the letter's theology are the same divine Savior.

5.synthesis: Paul explains why he left Titus in Crete: to put in order what remains and appoint elders in every town, as Paul directed (v.5). An elder must be above reproach — husband of one wife, with believing children who are not open to charges of debauchery or insubordination (v.6). For an overseer, as God's steward, must be above reproach — not arrogant, not quick-tempered, not addicted to wine, not violent, not greedy for gain; but hospitable, a lover of good, self-controlled, upright, holy, and disciplined (vv.7–8). He must hold firm to the trustworthy word as taught, so that he may give instruction in sound doctrine and also rebuke those who contradict it (v.9).
5.historical_context: Chrysostom frames the whole section with a moral contrast: the whole life of men in ancient times was one of action and contention; we by contrast lead a life of indolence. The point is that leadership requires the strenuous virtues — the elder is not appointed for ease but for the exacting work of ordering a church in difficult circumstances. Matthew Henry notes that the qualification list identifies both what elders should be and what they should not be, and that the negative list is particularly targeted at the temptations specific to Cretan culture (violence, greed, quick temper). The holding firm to the trustworthy word of v.9 is not optional: it is the doctrinal anchor that makes all the practical virtues of vv.7–8 coherent rather than merely social niceness.
5.literary_structure: The section transitions from the personal qualification list for the elder's household (v.6) to the character qualifications for the overseer as God's steward (vv.7–8) to the one doctrinal requirement (v.9). The household test parallels 1 Timothy 3:4–5: the domestic sphere is the proving ground for the public charge. God's steward is the key designation for the overseer: he manages not his own household but God's, which raises both the dignity and the accountability of the role. The doctrinal requirement of v.9 closes the list by naming the one ability that makes everything else possible: the capacity to teach sound doctrine and silence those who contradict it.
5.theological_application: Gill on holding fast the faithful word: the elder must hold the Gospel firmly, both believing it himself and preaching it to others; he must hold it fast against all opposition. The two-sided purpose of v.9 — to give instruction and to rebuke those who contradict — identifies the elder's role as both positive (edifying the community through sound teaching) and negative (defending the community from false teaching). The combination is essential in the Cretan context where many were willing to undermine the teaching for sordid gain (v.11). A man who can teach but not rebuke, or rebuke but not teach, is only half-equipped for the work.
5.christology: The trustworthy word the elder must hold fast is the word about Christ — the apostolic deposit entrusted to Paul and through him to Titus and the elders Titus appoints. Gill reads the faithful word as the Gospel in its full scope: the doctrine of grace, of Christ's person and work, of justification and sanctification. The elder who holds this word is holding Christ himself as the content of his teaching, and his ability to give instruction and refute opposition derives from the authority of the one whose word he holds. The chain of teaching — from Paul to Titus to elders to congregations — is the chain along which Christ continues to be proclaimed in Crete.

10.synthesis: For there are many who are insubordinate, empty talkers and deceivers, especially those of the circumcision party — who must be silenced, since they are upsetting whole families by teaching what they ought not to teach, for the sake of shameful gain (vv.10–11). One of the Cretans' own prophets said they are always liars, evil beasts, lazy gluttons — and this testimony is true (vv.12–13). Therefore rebuke them sharply, so that they may be sound in the faith, not devoting themselves to Jewish myths or commands of people who turn away from the truth (vv.13–14). To the pure, all things are pure; but to the defiled and unbelieving, nothing is pure — they profess to know God but deny him by their works, being detestable and disobedient and unfit for any good work (vv.15–16).
10.historical_context: Chrysostom devotes significant attention to the citation from Epimenides — the Cretan philosopher and poet who wrote that Cretans are always liars, evil beasts, lazy gluttons. He explains that Paul is not endorsing everything Epimenides wrote but that this particular characterization, which the Cretan's own tradition acknowledged, serves to justify the sharp rebuke Paul mandates. Gill on stopping their mouths: by reasons and arguments from the word of God, as Christ silenced the Sadducees and Pharisees and as Saul confounded the Damascus Jews. The circumcision party — Jewish Christians insisting on Torah observance and supplementing the gospel with the traditions of the elders — are the specific opponents behind the false teachers. Gill on to the pure all things are pure: the apostle is denying the Jewish legal traditions about ceremonial impurity; in Christ, the ceremonial law's impurity distinctions have been set aside, and conscience defiled by sin cannot be cleansed by ritual observance.
10.literary_structure: The section escalates: from the description of the false teachers (vv.10–11) through the cultural confirmation of their character (vv.12–13a) to the mandate to rebuke sharply (v.13b) to the basis for the rebuke in the purity principle (vv.14–15) to the final verdict on those who profess but deny (v.16). The Epimenides citation is not merely an ad hominem but a literary move that inverts the opponents' likely self-presentation: they profess superior knowledge of God's will through the Jewish traditions, but even their own cultural tradition acknowledged the habitual dishonesty of the Cretan character they are manifesting.
10.theological_application: Gill on to the pure all things are pure (v.15): the apostle is not teaching a general moral permissiveness but a specific counter to the false teachers' food-and-purity regulations. The person renewed by the Spirit of God has a purified conscience; external rituals cannot add to or subtract from that inner purity. The defiled and unbelieving who find everything impure are those whose consciences remain uncleansed — for them, the ritual system cannot cure the underlying defilement. Matthew Henry applies the profess-but-deny verdict of v.16 as the searching final word: there is a practical atheism that consists not in denying God's existence but in living as if his commands carried no real authority — a description that fits both the Cretan false teachers and every nominal professor in every generation.
10.christology: The Jewish myths and commands of people who turn away from the truth (v.14) are a turning away from the truth about Christ — the word the faithful elder holds (v.9) and the gospel Paul was entrusted to proclaim (v.3). The pure-to-the-pure principle points toward the one who is himself the source of the inner purity that the ritual law could not provide: Christ, who cleanses the conscience from dead works to serve the living God (Hebrews 9:14). Those who profess to know God but deny him by their works are denying not merely a doctrinal proposition but the living person in whom all the fullness of God dwells. The christological stakes of false teaching are always the person and work of Christ himself.
